threats.title: PEMCertChainLib notBefore Date Tag Validation Logic Error Accepts Invalid Certificates
threats.hypothesis_description: The notBefore date tag validation in PEMCertChainLib.decodeCert contains a logic error. The condition `(notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag == 0x18)` simplifies to `(notBeforeTag == 0x18)`, which rejects valid GeneralizedTime (0x18) tags while accepting any invalid tag (e.g., 0x05, 0x19). An attacker can craft a certificate with an arbitrary notBefore tag that passes validation. X509DateUtils.toTimestamp would parse garbage bytes, potentially producing notBefore=0, making the certificate appear perpetually valid from epoch start.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code bug in certificate validation logic that directly impacts SGX attestation security. Any on-chain actor can submit a quote with a crafted certificate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Condition `(notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag == 0x18)` simplifies to `(notBeforeTag == 0x18)` — inverted logic; should be `(notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag != 0x18)`

preconditions: Attacker can submit a quote containing a certificate with non-standard notBefore tag
The certificate chain must otherwise be valid (signed by trusted CA)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Invalid ASN.1 tag types for notBefore field bypass validation, causing X509DateUtils.toTimestamp to parse garbage into a timestamp
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — code bug present in all deployments

operational_questions: What timestamp does X509DateUtils.toTimestamp produce for garbage bytes with invalid tag types?
Can a certificate with notBefore=0 and a valid notAfter pass the expiry check: block.timestamp > 0 && block.timestamp < notAfter?
Does this allow acceptance of otherwise-expired or not-yet-valid certificates?
validation_requirements: Craft a certificate with notBefore tag 0x05 and verify it passes decodeCert
Test that valid GeneralizedTime (0x18) notBefore certificates are rejected
Verify end-to-end attestation with crafted certificate

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Craft a PCK certificate with notBefore using an invalid ASN.1 tag (e.g., 0x05)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Ensure the rest of the certificate chain is valid (signed correctly)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Submit a quote containing this certificate to verifyAttestation or verifyParsedQuote
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The invalid tag passes validation, X509DateUtils interprets garbage bytes, certificate appears valid

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: SGX attestation verification can accept certificates with manipulated validity periods
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Potential acceptance of expired or not-yet-valid SGX attestations, undermining the proof verification system
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires redeployment of PEMCertChainLib and all dependent contracts
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All SGX-based proof verification in the rollup

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Attestation verification calls with unusual certificate formats
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Successful attestation of quotes with suspicious certificate validity dates
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Not easily detectable without deep certificate inspection

threats.title: Certificate Chain Verification Skips Root CA Expiry and Self-Signature Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: In _verifyCertChain, the loop iterates through a 3-cert chain [PCK, Intermediate, Root]. At i=1 (intermediate cert), the issuer is certs[2] (root). When the root's pubkey hash matches ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH, the loop breaks with certChainCanBeTrusted=true WITHOUT processing i=2. This means the root certificate's own notBefore/notAfter expiry check and self-signature verification are never performed. An attacker can use an expired root CA certificate.
threats.investigation_rationale: The root CA certificate expiry is never validated, which violates the invariant that all certificates in the chain must be within their validity period.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop breaks at i=1 when issuerPubKeyHash == ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH; never reaches i=2 to verify root cert's own notBefore/notAfter

preconditions: Root CA certificate has expired (notAfter < block.timestamp)
Root CA public key still matches ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH
PCK and intermediate certs are valid and not expired

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Verification loop terminates early on root CA pubkey match before checking root cert validity
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After root CA certificate expires but key remains the same

operational_questions: When does the current Intel SGX Root CA certificate expire?
Would Intel revoke the root CA key before expiry?
Does the root cert's serial number appear in revocation lists?
validation_requirements: Construct a quote using an expired root CA cert and verify it passes _verifyCertChain
Confirm that the root cert's self-signature is never checked

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain or construct a quote chain where the root CA certificate has expired
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Submit the quote for attestation verification
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The loop breaks at i=1 after matching root pubkey, skipping root cert expiry check
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attestation passes despite expired root certificate

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Attestation system accepts proofs anchored to expired trust root
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Weakened trust model for SGX-based proof verification

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Successful attestations with expired root CA

threats.title: TimelockTokenPool costToWithdraw Precision Loss via Division-Before-Multiplication
threats.hypothesis_description: In getMyGrantSummary, costToWithdraw is calculated as `(amountUnlocked / 1e18) * costPerToken - costPaid`. The division by 1e18 before multiplication truncates up to 1e18-1 tokens worth of cost per withdrawal. For grants with non-zero costPerToken, each withdrawal loses up to costPerToken * (1e18-1) / 1e18 in cost payment. While each recipient can only withdraw from a single grant, strategic withdrawal timing can maximize the precision loss benefit.
threats.investigation_rationale: Division before multiplication is a classic Solidity precision loss pattern. Here it directly reduces the cost recipients must pay, creating a quantifiable value leak.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `uint128 _amountUnlocked = amountUnlocked / 1e18; costToWithdraw = _amountUnlocked * r.grant.costPerToken - r.costPaid;` — division before multiplication truncates

preconditions: Grant has non-zero costPerToken
Recipient has unlocked tokens available for withdrawal

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Integer division truncation in cost calculation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — affects all recipients with costPerToken > 0

operational_questions: What are typical costPerToken values in production grants?
How much total cost leakage is possible across all grants?
Can recipients optimize withdrawal timing to maximize precision loss?
validation_requirements: Calculate maximum cost savings per withdrawal for typical grant parameters
Quantify total protocol loss across all expected grants

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Recipient waits until amountUnlocked has maximum sub-1e18 remainder (e.g., X * 1e18 + 1e18 - 1)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call withdraw() — the truncation drops up to 1e18-1 tokens from the cost basis
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Recipient pays less costToken than intended for the same token amount

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Cost token accounting becomes inaccurate over time
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix formula

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Discrepancy between expected cost payments and actual cost payments
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Cost token balance in sharedVault lower than expected

threats.title: ERC20Airdrop2 Permissionless Forced Token Withdrawal for Any User
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20Airdrop2.withdraw(address user) has no access control — any caller can force token withdrawal for any user during the withdrawal window. The function sends the user's time-based allowance directly to their address via IERC20.transferFrom(vault, user, amount). While tokens go to the rightful owner, this forces withdrawal timing, can drain vault allowance, and removes the user's choice of when to receive tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Permissionless withdrawal violates the principle that users should control when they receive assets. This can have tax implications, gas cost implications, and may interact badly with certain recipient contracts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function withdraw(address user) external ongoingWithdrawals — no access control, anyone can trigger withdrawal for any user

preconditions: Claim period has ended (block.timestamp >= claimEnd)
Withdrawal window is active (block.timestamp <= claimEnd + withdrawalWindow)
User has claimed tokens (claimedAmount[user] > 0)
Vault has approved airdrop contract for sufficient tokens

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No msg.sender == user check in withdraw function

operational_questions: Is forcing early withdrawal harmful to users (tax events, accounting)?
Can batch forced withdrawals drain vault allowance before legitimate users act?
Are there smart contract recipients that could be harmed by forced ERC20 receipt?
validation_requirements: Confirm any EOA can call withdraw(otherUser) successfully
Test batch forced withdrawals across multiple users

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait until withdrawal window opens (claimEnd < block.timestamp)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Enumerate all users who have claimed via claimedAmount events
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call withdraw(user) for each user, forcing immediate token transfer
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All users receive tokens at attacker-chosen timing

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users lose control over withdrawal timing; potential tax and accounting impacts
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low — tokens go to correct recipients

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple withdraw() calls from same address for different users
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mass forced withdrawals in single block
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Third-party address triggering withdrawals for many users

threats.title: AutomataDcapV3Attestation Missing Ownership Transfer Creates Irrecoverable Admin Lock
threats.hypothesis_description: AutomataDcapV3Attestation uses a simple `owner` state variable set in the constructor with no transfer, renounce, or update mechanism. All admin functions (setMrSigner, setMrEnclave, configureTcbInfoJson, configureQeIdentityJson, addRevokedCertSerialNum, removeRevokedCertSerialNum, toggleLocalReportCheck) are gated by onlyOwner. If the owner key is lost, all configuration becomes permanently frozen. Unlike EssentialContract-derived contracts that have Ownable2Step, this contract's admin access is irrecoverable.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a missing functionality defect. Every other admin contract in the protocol has ownership transfer capability. The attestation contract controls critical security configuration but lacks this basic operational safety mechanism.
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol: AutomataDcapV3Attestation deployed via `new` with owner set to deployer. No ownership transfer call exists in the deployment script or the contract code.

proof_of_signal.pattern: constructor sets `owner = msg.sender`; no transferOwnership or setOwner function exists in the contract

preconditions: Owner key is lost or compromised (for operational impact)
Intel updates TCB info or revokes certificates (for configuration staleness)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Contract lacks any owner transfer function despite having 7+ onlyOwner admin functions
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Single EOA permanently controls all attestation configuration

operational_questions: Is the owner address a multisig or EOA?
Is there a migration plan if the owner key needs to change?
How frequently must TCB info be updated?
validation_requirements: Confirm no ownership transfer function exists
Verify deployment script does not attempt ownership transfer

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: N/A — this is a missing functionality defect, not an active attack
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Operational failure occurs when owner key is lost or Intel updates infrastructure
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All admin configuration functions become permanently inaccessible
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attestation contract must be redeployed and re-registered

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Permanent loss of attestation configuration control on key loss
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unable to update TCB info, revoke certificates, or change trusted enclaves
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires redeployment and AddressManager update (governed by timelock)

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When owner key is lost or needs rotation

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Stale TCB info or inability to process certificate revocations

threats.title: PEMCertChainLib._findTcb Array Out-of-Bounds Revert When PCESVN Is Not Last SVN Entry
threats.hypothesis_description: In _findTcb, cpusvns is allocated with size SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE (16) but the loop iterates SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1 (17) times. The PCESVN entry is stored in a separate variable when its OID matches. If PCESVN is NOT the last entry in the DER sequence (i.e., not at index 16), the 17th iteration writes to cpusvns[16], causing an out-of-bounds revert in Solidity 0.8.x. Additionally, cpusvns entries after the PCESVN position are mapped to wrong indices, causing incorrect TCB level matching.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete array bounds bug. While current Intel PCK certs may place PCESVN last, any format change would break all attestation verification permanently.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop `for (uint256 i; i < SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1; ++i)` iterates 17 times; `cpusvns = new uint256[](SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE)` has size 16; `cpusvns[i] = cpusvn` at i=16 causes OOB if PCESVN wasn't at that index

preconditions: PCK certificate has PCESVN OID at a position other than the last (index 16)
Certificate is otherwise valid and submitted for attestation

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Array size 16 with 17 iterations; relies on PCESVN being the last entry to avoid OOB
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: If Intel changes SVN ordering in PCK certificates

operational_questions: Is the PCESVN always the last SVN component in Intel PCK certificates?
Has Intel ever changed the ordering of SVN components?
Even without OOB, does the index-skipping produce incorrect cpusvns mapping?
validation_requirements: Parse real Intel PCK certificates to verify PCESVN position
Craft a test certificate with PCESVN at different positions and verify behavior

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain or craft a PCK certificate where PCESVN OID appears before index 16
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Submit a quote containing this certificate
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The _findTcb function reverts at cpusvns[16] access, causing attestation DoS
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All attestation attempts with such certificates permanently fail

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete attestation verification failure for affected certificate formats

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Attestation verification reverts with array OOB
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden failure of all SGX attestations after Intel certificate update

threats.title: TimelockTokenPool Withdrawal Signature Lacks Domain Separator Enabling Cross-Chain Replay
threats.hypothesis_description: TimelockTokenPool.withdraw(address _to, bytes memory _sig) recovers the recipient from a signature of `keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to))`. This hash contains no chain ID, no contract address, no nonce, and no expiry. If the same recipient has grants in TimelockTokenPool instances on multiple chains, a signature captured from one chain can be replayed on another chain to force withdrawal to the same _to address. The on-chain replay within the same contract is mitigated by amountWithdrawn tracking (zero subsequent withdrawal), but cross-chain replay is unmitigated.
threats.investigation_rationale: Missing EIP-712 domain separator is a well-known vulnerability pattern. Here it concretely enables cross-chain replay of withdrawal signatures.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bytes32 hash = keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to)); — no chainId, no contract address, no nonce

preconditions: Same recipient address has grants in TimelockTokenPool on multiple chains
Recipient signs a withdrawal signature on one chain
Attacker observes the signature on-chain after execution

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Signed message contains only target address and a fixed string prefix
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After any withdrawal signature is used on any chain

operational_questions: Are TimelockTokenPool instances deployed on multiple chains with overlapping recipients?
Does the protocol documentation describe cross-chain deployment of token pools?
Is the _to address typically a user-controlled address or could it be a contract?
validation_requirements: Verify signature recovery works identically across chain instances
Test replay of a mainnet signature on an L2 deployment

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor chain A for withdraw(address,bytes) transactions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Extract _to and _sig from the executed transaction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call withdraw(_to, _sig) on chain B's TimelockTokenPool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Recipient's chain B tokens are sent to _to address, which may be controlled differently across chains

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Forced withdrawal on unintended chain; potential token loss if _to is a contract address controlled on one chain but not another
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Tokens cannot be recovered once sent

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After a signature is used on any chain

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Same signature appearing on multiple chains
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: withdraw(address,bytes) calls shortly after same call on different chain

threats.title: ERC20Airdrop2 Division-by-Zero DoS if Initialized with withdrawalWindow=0
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20Airdrop2.init does not validate that _withdrawalWindow > 0. In getBalance(), the calculation `balance * (block.timestamp.min(claimEnd + withdrawalWindow) - claimEnd) / withdrawalWindow` divides by withdrawalWindow. If withdrawalWindow is 0, every call to withdraw() reverts with division-by-zero, permanently locking all claimed airdrop tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Missing input validation on a critical initialization parameter. Once deployed with withdrawalWindow=0, all airdrop tokens become permanently locked with no recovery mechanism.
threats.config_evidence: ERC20Airdrop2.sol init() has no check for _withdrawalWindow > 0. No deployment script for ERC20Airdrop2 was provided to verify actual deployment parameters.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getBalance divides by withdrawalWindow; init() does not validate _withdrawalWindow > 0

preconditions: Contract initialized with _withdrawalWindow = 0
Users have claimed tokens during the claim period

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No validation of withdrawalWindow in init

operational_questions: What withdrawalWindow values are used in production deployments?
Is there any recovery mechanism (upgrade, owner action) to fix a zero withdrawalWindow?
validation_requirements: Deploy ERC20Airdrop2 with withdrawalWindow=0 and confirm withdraw reverts
Confirm no setter function exists for withdrawalWindow

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: N/A — this is a deployment configuration vulnerability
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If deployed with withdrawalWindow=0, all users who claim can never withdraw
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All claimed airdrop tokens are permanently locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Only recovery is contract upgrade via UUPS proxy pattern

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete permanent lock of all airdrop tokens
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires UUPS proxy upgrade by owner

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: All withdraw calls reverting with arithmetic error

threats.title: SigVerifyLib assert(success) Causes Gas-Draining Panic on ES256VERIFIER Failure
threats.hypothesis_description: SigVerifyLib.verifyES256Signature uses `assert(success)` after a staticcall to ES256VERIFIER. If ES256VERIFIER has no code deployed, is self-destructed, or reverts, the staticcall returns success=false. The `assert(success)` then triggers a Panic(0x01), consuming ALL remaining gas without refund. This makes every failed ES256 verification extremely expensive, creating a gas-griefing vector for attestation verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: assert() should be reserved for invariant checks that indicate bugs. External call failure is an expected condition that should use require(). Using assert() on external call results is a code defect that wastes user gas.
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol: `SigVerifyLib sigVerifyLib = new SigVerifyLib(address(p256Verifier));` — P256Verifier is deployed, but if destroyed later, the assert becomes a gas trap.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `(bool success, bytes memory ret) = ES256VERIFIER.staticcall(args); assert(success);` — assert on external call result consumes all gas on failure

preconditions: ES256VERIFIER contract is not deployed, destroyed, or malfunctions
User calls verifyAttestation or verifyParsedQuote triggering ES256 signature check

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: assert() used for external call success check instead of require()
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — depends on ES256VERIFIER contract state

operational_questions: Can P256Verifier be self-destructed?
What happens if the ES256VERIFIER address has no code?
How much gas is typically sent for attestation verification?
validation_requirements: Test verifyES256Signature with a non-existent ES256VERIFIER address
Measure gas consumption on assert failure vs require failure

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: If ES256VERIFIER becomes non-functional (destruction, chain migration)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Every attestation verification attempt consumes all provided gas
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users pay maximum gas for guaranteed failures
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Effective DoS on the entire attestation system

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All attestation verification becomes prohibitively expensive to attempt
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires redeployment of SigVerifyLib and AutomataDcapV3Attestation

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Attestation calls consuming maximum gas and reverting with Panic
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden increase in gas costs for attestation calls

threats.title: ERC20Airdrop Initialization Deploys with Zero Config — Claims Blocked Until Manual Setup
threats.hypothesis_description: The ERC20Airdrop deployment script initializes with `ERC20Airdrop.init(address(0), 0, 0, bytes32(0), bridgedTko, vaultAddress)` — claimStart=0, claimEnd=0, merkleRoot=0. The `ongoingClaim` modifier blocks all claims when merkleRoot == 0 or claimStart == 0 or claimEnd == 0. Claims require a subsequent setConfig() call. If the owner fails to call setConfig or misconfigures it, no users can claim. Additionally, using transferFrom without SafeERC20 for the token transfer could cause silent failures with non-standard ERC20 tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Deployment script shows a gap between initialization and operational readiness. The contract is deployed in a non-functional state, and the vault approval is also noted as TODO in the deployment script comments.
deployment_assumptions: Owner must call setConfig() to enable claims
Vault must approve airdrop contract as spender
threats.config_evidence: DeployERC20Airdrop.s.sol lines 35-42: init called with all-zero claim parameters; commented-out vault approval indicates manual post-deployment step required

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(token).transferFrom(vault, user, amount) — uses raw transferFrom not SafeERC20.safeTransferFrom; combined with zero-config deployment

preconditions: Owner fails to call setConfig() after deployment
OR vault fails to approve airdrop contract as token spender

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Deployment script initializes with non-functional parameters; vault approval is commented-out TODO

operational_questions: Is the vault approval automated in a separate deployment step?
How quickly after deployment is setConfig called?
Is the airdrop token truly standard ERC20 (returns bool on transfer)?
validation_requirements: Verify deployment flow includes setConfig and vault approval
Test claimAndDelegate with the actual bridged TKO token

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If owner deploys but forgets setConfig, no claims possible
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If vault approval is missing, claims succeed but transferFrom reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users unable to claim their airdrop allocation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Airdrop claims blocked until configuration completed
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low — owner calls setConfig and vault approves

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Zero merkleRoot on deployed airdrop contract

threats.title: AutomataDcapV3Attestation Owner Outside Governance — Deployer EOA Retains Permanent Control
threats.hypothesis_description: The deployment script (DeployOnL1.s.sol) deploys AutomataDcapV3Attestation with `new` (not proxied), and the constructor sets owner to msg.sender (the deployer EOA). The contract has no ownership transfer function. All other protocol contracts transfer ownership to the timelock governance system. The attestation contract — which controls all SGX attestation configuration — remains under the deployer's direct EOA control, outside the governance/timelock system. A compromised deployer key grants permanent, irrevocable control over attestation trust configuration.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete deployment gap where the attestation contract's admin is decoupled from the rest of the protocol's governance. The deployer EOA has powers equivalent to the timelock but without governance oversight.
deployment_assumptions: Deployer EOA retains permanent ownership of attestation contract
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol: `AutomataDcapV3Attestation automateDcapV3Attestation = new AutomataDcapV3Attestation(address(sigVerifyLib), address(pemCertChainLib));` — no transferOwnership call; contract has no such function

proof_of_signal.pattern: owner = msg.sender in constructor; no transferOwnership function exists; deployment script never transfers ownership to timelock

preconditions: Deployer EOA private key is compromised
Attacker uses the key to reconfigure attestation trust

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: All other contracts transfer ownership to timelock; this one does not and cannot
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Single EOA with no governance oversight

operational_questions: Is the deployer EOA key secured in hardware/multisig?
Has the team considered the risk of this key being separate from governance?
Would adding a malicious MrEnclave immediately allow forged proofs?
validation_requirements: Verify the deployer address on mainnet and its security posture
Assess impact of malicious configureTcbInfoJson or setMrEnclave calls

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromise the deployer EOA private key (phishing, key extraction, insider)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call setMrEnclave(attacker_enclave, true) to whitelist malicious enclave
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call configureTcbInfoJson with permissive TCB levels
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Submit forged SGX attestations that pass verification

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete control over SGX attestation trust model
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Ability to forge SGX proofs, potentially compromising rollup security
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Must redeploy attestation contract and update AddressManager via timelock governance

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unexpected setMrEnclave/configureTcbInfoJson calls from deployer EOA
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: New trusted enclaves or TCB configurations appearing without governance proposal
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Admin calls outside normal operational schedule

threats.title: TaikoL2EIP1559Configurable Owner Can Disable Dynamic Basefee via gasExcess=0
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoL2EIP1559Configurable.setConfigAndExcess allows the owner to set gasExcess to 0, which permanently disables the EIP-1559 dynamic base fee (basefee returns 1 wei). In _calc1559BaseFee, the check `if (gasExcess > 0)` skips all computation when gasExcess is 0, and `_parentGasUsed` is only added when gasExcess > 0, so gasExcess can never organically recover. Alternatively, setting gasExcess to type(uint64).max spikes basefee to maximum, making L2 transactions prohibitively expensive.
threats.investigation_rationale: The owner has unrestricted ability to manipulate the core economic parameter of the L2 network. Setting gasExcess=0 creates an irreversible state where basefee is permanently 1 wei.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `if (gasExcess > 0)` gates all basefee computation; when gasExcess=0, excess never accumulates, basefee permanently returns 1

preconditions: Owner (via cross-chain governance) calls setConfigAndExcess with _newGasExcess = 0
OR attacker compromises the cross-chain owner account

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: gasExcess=0 creates irreversible state; no minimum gasExcess validation

operational_questions: What are the economic consequences of basefee=1 on L2?
Could this enable spam attacks that degrade L2 performance?
Is there monitoring for gasExcess changes?
validation_requirements: Confirm setConfigAndExcess(config, 0) makes basefee permanently 1
Verify gasExcess cannot recover from 0 through normal anchor operations

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromise cross-chain owner or governance mechanism
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: gasExcess becomes 0; all subsequent anchors produce basefee=1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: L2 network becomes vulnerable to spam with minimal cost

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Network spam enabled at minimal cost; potential chain congestion
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires owner to call setConfigAndExcess with non-zero gasExcess

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Governance control or owner key compromise

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: gasExcess set to 0 or extreme values
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: basefee dropping to 1 across all blocks
